risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition." Comment: This describes a truly random allocation sequence generated by computer random numbers, which meets the low-risk criterion for random sequence generation.

Quote: "Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition. After all participants had completed baseline assessments, this information was given to study staff who informed participants of their group condition." Comment: This indicates that allocation was generated and held by a statistician, with group assignment communicated only after baseline assessments were completed. This is an explicit mechanism preventing recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment, so allocation concealment is judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were not blinded to study hypotheses." and "Participants randomly assigned to the waitlist group were offered the mindfulness program after completion of all posttreatment assessments." Comment: This was an unblinded waitlist-controlled behavioral intervention, so participants and personnel delivering the intervention would have known allocation. Because the main outcomes included self-report measures (e.g. stress, anxiety, eating behaviors) and treatment adherence, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence outcomes and behavior, making performance bias high overall.

Quote: "Study nurses, blind to participant condition, performed the anthropometric and body composition assessments and blood draws. Research assistants administered the computerized questionnaires and provided instructions for the home saliva sampling procedure, but were not blind to participant condition at posttreatment assessments." Comment: The key outcome assessment for this review includes weight/body composition and related biological outcomes, which were measured by blinded study nurses or automated laboratory methods (blood draws/analytes, DEXA). These outcomes are objective and unlikely to be influenced by lack of blinding. Although research assistants were not blinded for questionnaires and home saliva instructions, the paper explicitly states the nurses assessing the main physical outcomes were blinded, and the objective measurements reduce detection bias.

Quote: "Five treatment and two control participants were lost to followup for the primary analysis" and "Assuming participants lost to followup did not change over time, missing data at postintervention were imputed using preintervention values." Comment: Attrition was modest overall (7/47 randomized, about 15%) and reasonably balanced between groups (5 vs 2). The paper also used a prespecified intention-to-treat approach with baseline-value imputation for missing postintervention data, which is plausible in this small pilot study and does not suggest a strong risk of attrition bias.
